ground_truth.title: Specs Are Back, But We're Missing the Tools
ground_truth.author: Pierre Zemb
ground_truth.main_content: I truly think LLMs are changing how we write software. For me, it's been a massive productivity boost. I can ask Claude to read some piece of code and explain it to me, or make a quick PoC of something, or refactor stuff that would take me hours. But here's what I keep running into: we're still using natural language to prompt, correct, and guide LLMs. Vague instructions produce vague code. The bottleneck isn't writing code anymore, it's knowing what to write in the first place. What changed is that LLMs can actually read specifications now. And suddenly, specs aren't dead documents anymore. They're instructions that can be executed. I've found two modes that actually work: Generation and Validation. I tried spec-kit a while ago and found it pretty useful. What it does well is guide you through a structured chain of prompts. The generated code was actually good, because spec-kit forced me to build the right context first. Here's where I hit a wall though. English-based specs work great for user stories and acceptance criteria, but for algorithms and system behavior? Natural language gets ambiguous really fast. I started looking at formal methods. TLA+ is the classic choice, but the notation felt like another language to maintain. Then a friend suggested Fizzbee. It's based on Starlark, a Python dialect. Model checking without the TLA+ notation. Fizzbee is great for what it does. For algorithms and concurrency, model checking feels like the Rust compiler but for higher-level design. But most software isn't distributed algorithms. Most of what we build is about storing data in databases, sending messages to queues, calling other services, transforming inputs into outputs. I keep thinking about what this tool would need to be. It should be compact, short enough to fit in an LLM's context window without eating thousands of tokens. It should work at both levels: service interactions and function behavior. It should be the common language that both the team and the LLM can read, write, and reason about. Most importantly, it should be verifiable. Not just documentation that sits there, but something that can actually validate whether an implementation matches what we said it would do. The feedback loop that specs never had.

But here's what I keep running into: we're still using natural language to prompt, correct, and guide LLMs.
What changed is that LLMs can actually read specifications now. And suddenly, specs aren't dead documents anymore.
I tried spec-kit a while ago and found it pretty useful. What it does well is guide you through a structured chain of prompts.
Most importantly, it should be verifiable. Not just documentation that sits there, but something that can actually validate whether an implementation matches what said it would do.
